denial_letter_text:
Dear Mr. Member,

Aetna has reviewed the prior authorization request for transcatheter aortic valve replacement (TAVR) for aortic stenosis. After review, the request is denied at this time.

Plan policy requires that a less costly and established treatment modality be used when available. Under our 2017 coverage policy for aortic valve replacement, surgical aortic valve replacement is the expected modality for members who are candidates for surgery, and transcatheter replacement is reserved for members established to be at high surgical risk. Based on the information reviewed, high surgical risk has not been established, so the surgical modality is the appropriate option at this stage.

This determination was made by Dr. M. Alvarez. The notice does not state the reviewer's specialty, a license number, or any credentials for this determination.

If you disagree with this determination, you may file an internal appeal by contacting Member Services at the number on your identification card within the timeframe stated in your plan documents. You may submit additional clinical information with your appeal, and a different reviewer will evaluate the appeal. You may also request a copy of the specific coverage policy relied upon in this determination, and you may include any additional records you believe are relevant.

This determination addresses coverage under your plan only and is not medical advice.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 68-year-old man with severe symptomatic aortic stenosis, with an aortic valve area of 0.8 cm squared, a mean transvalvular gradient of 46 mmHg, a peak velocity of 4.3 m/s, and two episodes of exertional syncope. His cardiologist and the heart team recommended transcatheter aortic valve replacement. The denial relies on the plan's 2017 aortic valve replacement coverage policy, which restricted transcatheter replacement to members at high surgical risk; the current 2020 ACC/AHA valvular heart disease guideline supersedes that policy and supports transcatheter aortic valve replacement as a reasonable alternative to surgery across the surgical-risk spectrum for appropriate candidates, including older adults. The denial names Dr. M. Alvarez as the reviewer but lists no specialty, license number, or credentials, so the member cannot verify the reviewer's qualifications or identity against the state medical board.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field; severe symptomatic aortic stenosis is cardiology; TAVR realistic; modality_substitution (demand SAVR before TAVR) is the rationale; 68M in 56-70.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Aetna demanding SAVR before TAVR is realistic; the superseded 2017-vs-2020 valve policy and the named-but-uncredentialed reviewer are genuine exploitable gaps.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Correct Aetna branding and procedural cadence, no wrong-insurer tells, but no named Aetna CPB/InterQual fingerprint.
insurer_voice.improvement: Cite an Aetna Clinical Policy Bulletin or InterQual criteria.

denial_logic.reasoning: Authentically shoddy modality_substitution: demands cheaper/established SAVR, asserts high surgical risk not established without naming the threshold tools; exploitable.

clinical_realism.reasoning: 68M severe symptomatic AS: valve area 0.8, mean gradient 46, peak velocity 4.3, syncope; Class I indication; TAVR appropriate; 2020 ACC/AHA citation accurate; no biology violations.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: TAVR is guideline-recommended for severe symptomatic aortic stenosis; exact match.

diversity_delta.reasoning: Aortic stenosis / TAVR with modality_substitution is a unique dx+tx+denial point; no cardiology neighbour involves aortic stenosis or valve replacement.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.179 (nearest=case_153_aetna_priorauth), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: Curative structural-heart intervention dispute; no banned topics; adult 68M; Aetna commercial with no Medicare/Medicaid framing.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Gender, diagnosis, treatment, 2017-policy, and reviewer details stable across documents; guideline-vs-policy tension is appealability not contradiction.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Formal insurer boilerplate and dense clinical recitation; no GPT vocabulary, listicles, or meta-commentary.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural Aetna register; clinical context plain analytic, no pleading.

financial_auditor.reasoning: Cost referenced only as coverage-modality rationale; no patient liability dollar figures; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation.

legal_auditor.reasoning: Internal appeal present; omitted external-review/ERISA language and buried window are realistic attackable imperfections; no hallucinated statutes; no Medicare framing.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 68M with severe calcific aortic stenosis is epidemiologically textbook; consistent male pronouns/'Mr.'; no stereotyping.

scope_guard.reasoning: Aetna in-scope, fully-insured commercial, adult 68, member-facing internal appeal only, no live-filing language.

date_sanity.reasoning: No contradictions; policy/guideline years (2017, 2020) consistent but no absolute denial/request dates or numeric appeal window.
date_sanity.improvement: Add absolute 2026 dates and a numeric appeal window.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Generic un-named '2017 coverage policy' referenced by owner and year, no module/code; no hallucinated alphanumeric code; realistically vague.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Superseded 2017 policy vs current 2020 ACC/AHA guideline, named-but-uncredentialed reviewer, and ignored Class-I indication; easy multi-vector appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Denial cites a superseded 2017 policy; the current 2020 ACC/AHA guideline supports TAVR across the surgical-risk spectrum, undermining the high-risk-only premise
Reviewer Dr. M. Alvarez has no listed specialty, license number, or credentials, so qualifications cannot be verified
Patient plainly meets standard of care for intervention (severe symptomatic AS with syncope, heart-team TAVR recommendation), which the denial ignores
strong_defenses: The denial invokes a real least-costly-modality principle and offers proper appeal rights, a second reviewer, and access to the coverage policy
